concepts.title: Gauss Law
concepts.conceptBody:
Gauss Law

This law gives the relation between the electric flux through any closed hypothetical surface (called a Gaussian surface) and the charge enclosed by the surface. It states "the electric flux $\left( {{\phi }_{\mathbf{E}}} \right)$ through any closed surface is equal to $\frac{1}{{{\epsilon }_{0}}}$ times the 'net' charged enclosed".

That is,

${{\phi }_{\text{E}}}=\oint \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\vec{E}\cdot \overrightarrow{\text{ds}}=\frac{\sum ~\text{q}}{{{\epsilon }_{0}}}$

where $\text{ }\!\!\Sigma\!\!\text{ }q$ denotes the algebraic sum of all the charges enclosed by the surface. If there are several charges $+{{q}_{1}},+{{q}_{2}},+{{q}_{3}},-{{q}_{4}},-{{q}_{5}}$ inside the Gaussian surface then

Remarks : 

${{\text{ }\!\!\Sigma\!\!\text{ }}_{\text{q}}}={{\text{q}}_{1}}+{{\text{q}}_{2}}+{{\text{q}}_{3}}-{{\text{q}}_{4}}-{{\text{q}}_{5}}$

(1) Gaussian surface is an imaginary closed surface.

(2) Gaussian surface cannot be passed through a discrete charges but can be passed through continuous charge distributions.

Note : The electric field is directed in radial direction for spherically symmetry charge distribution hence the use of Gauss's law simplifies the position.

concepts.title: Electric Field Due to Uniform Charged Non Conducting Sphere
concepts.conceptBody:
Electric Field Due to Uniform Charged Non Conducting Sphere

(a) For an internal point 

Consider a gaussion surface at a distance from the centre inside the surface

Note that the direction of the electric field will be directed radially outward

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\phi ~=\int ~\vec{E}\cdot \text{d}\vec{s}=\text{E}\int ~\text{d}s=\text{E}\left( 4\pi {{\text{r}}^{2}} \right) \\ \text{E}\left(4\pi {{\text{r}}^{2}} \right) ~=\frac{\rho \left(\frac{4}{3}\pi {{\text{r}}^{3}} \right)}{{{\epsilon }_{0}}} \\ \therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\mathbf{E} ~=\left(\frac{\rho \text{r}}{3{{\in }_{0}}} \right) \\ \end{array}$

when $\text{r}>\text{R}$

(b) For an external point

$\text{E}\left( 4\pi {{\text{r}}^{2}} \right)=\left( \frac{\text{Q}}{{{\epsilon }_{0}}} \right)$

$\mathbf{E}=\frac{\mathbf{Q}}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}{{\text{r}}^{2}}}$

when $r>R$ Illustration

Find the field intensity inside a thick conducting shell of inner and outer radii, $\mathbf{R}$ and $2\mathbf{R}$, whose volume charge density varies as, $\rho =\left( \frac{{{\rho }_{0}}r}{R} \right)$

Solution : Using gauss's law by making a gaussian surface at a distance $\text{r}$ from the centre

${{q}_{\text{inside }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\rho \left( 4\pi {{r}^{2}} \right)dr=\frac{{{\rho }_{0}}4\pi {{r}^{3}}dr}{R}$

Hence according to gauss's Law

$\text{E}\left( 4\pi {{\text{r}}^{2}} \right)=\int _{\text{R}}^{\text{r}}\left( \frac{{{\rho }_{0}}\text{r}\left( 4\pi {{\text{r}}^{2}} \right)\text{dr}}{\frac{\text{R}}{{{\epsilon }_{0}}}} \right)$

$\therefore $$E=\frac{{{\rho }_{0}}\left( {{r}^{4}}-{{R}^{4}} \right)}{\text{R}{{\text{r}}^{2}}\left( 4{{\epsilon }_{0}} \right)}$

concepts.title: Electric Field Intensity Due to Infinite Long Wire
concepts.conceptBody:
Electric Field Intensity Due to Infinite Long Wire

Assume a coaxial cylindrical gausian surface, at a distance $r$.

Charge enclosed by it $\left( q \right)=\left( \lambda l \right)$,

Hence, $\text{E}\left( 2\pi \text{rl} \right)=\frac{\left( \lambda \text{l} \right)}{{{\epsilon }_{0}}}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\mathbf{E}=\frac{\lambda }{2\pi {{\in }_{0}}r}$

concepts.title: Electric Field Intensity Due to Charged Non-Conducting Infinite Sheet
concepts.conceptBody:
Electric Field Intensity Due to Charged Non-Conducting Infinite Sheet 

Let us consider an infinite non-conducting charged plane sheet having a surface charge density $\sigma \frac{\text{C}}{{{\text{m}}^{2}}}$

Now, assume a cylindrical gaussian surface through the plane sheet.

The area of each end is $\pi {{r}^{2}}$. By symmetry, the electric field intensity at all points on either side near the sheet will be perpendicular to the sheet, directed outward (if the sheet is positively charged). Thus $\vec{E}$ is perpendicular to the plane ends of the cylinder and parallel to the curved surface.

Therefore, flux through the two plane ends is

${{\phi }_{\text{E}}}=\int ~\text{E}\cdot \pi {{\text{r}}^{2}}+\int ~\text{E}\cdot \pi {{\text{r}}^{2}}=2\int ~\text{E}\cdot \pi {{\text{r}}^{2}}$

$\phi =\frac{{{\text{q}}_{\text{inside }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}}{{{\epsilon }_{0}}}=\frac{\sigma \left( \pi {{\text{r}}^{2}} \right)}{{{\epsilon }_{0}}}=2\text{E}\cdot \left( \pi {{\text{r}}^{2}} \right)$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\mathbf{E}=\frac{\sigma }{2{{\epsilon }_{0}}}$

concepts.title: Electric Field Intensity Due to Uniform Charged Infinitely Long Cylinder
concepts.conceptBody:
Electric Field Intensity Due to Uniform Charged Infinitely Long Cylinder

(a) For an internal point 

Since charge is uniformly distributed through out the volume of cylinder of radius R. For finding the electric field at a distance $(\text{r}<\text{R})$ from the centre, we choose a cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius $r$, co-axial with the charge distribution.

The magnitude $\text{E}$ of the electric field has the same value at every point on the Gaussian surface and the direction of $\vec{E}$ is radial at every point on the lateral surface. Since, direction of electric field on the circular ends is taken to be zero. So, applying Gauss's law

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \oint \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ E}.\text{ds}=\text{E}\left( 2\pi \text{rl} \right)=\frac{\rho \left( \pi {{\text{r}}^{2}}\text{l} \right)}{{{\epsilon }_{0}}} \\ {} & ~\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ E}=\frac{\rho \text{r}}{2{{\epsilon }_{0}}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ in }\!\!~\!\!\text{ vector }\!\!~\!\!\vec{E}=\frac{\rho \vec{r}}{2{{\epsilon }_{0}}} \\ \end{array}$

(b) For an external point 

Similarly, as done above consider a gaussian surface co-axial with the uniformly charged cylinder at radius $\text{r}(\text{r}>\text{R})$

Thus, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\! \vec{E}\cdot \left( 2\pi \text{rl} \right)=\frac{\rho \left( \pi {{\text{R}}^{2}} \right)\text{l}}{{{\epsilon }_{0}}}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ E}=\left( \frac{\rho {{\text{R}}^{2}}}{2{{\epsilon }_{0}}\text{r}} \right)$

in vector form

$\vec{E}=\left( \frac{\rho {{R}^{2}}}{2{{\in }_{0}}{{\text{r}}^{2}}} \right)\vec{r}$

Plot of electric field v/s distance from the axis

concepts.title: Different Forms of Gauss Law
concepts.conceptBody:
Differential form of Gauss's Law 

Derivation : 

Consider the flux through an infinitesimal volume is space. Net flux through the cube

$=\left[ -{{\text{E}}_{\text{X}}}\left( \text{dydz} \right)+\left( {{\text{E}}_{\text{X}}}+\text{d}{{\text{E}}_{\text{X}}} \right)\text{dydz} \right]$$+\left[ -{{\text{E}}_{\text{y}}}\left( \text{dzdx} \right)+\left( \text{Ey}+\text{dEy} \right)\left( \text{dzdx} \right] \right.$ $+\left[ -\text{Ez}\left( \text{dxdy} \right)+\left( \text{Ez}+\text{dEz} \right)\left( \text{dxdy} \right) \right]$

$=\left( \text{dEx}dydz \right)+\left( \text{dEy}dzdx \right)+d\text{Ez}\left( \text{d}x\text{dy} \right)$

Using Gauss law

$\left( dExdydz \right)+\left( dEydxdz \right)+\left( dEzdxdy \right)=\frac{\rho}{\epsilon_0}$

$\left( \frac{dEx}{dx} \right)+\left( \frac{dEy}{dy} \right)+\left( \frac{dEz}{dz} \right)=\frac{\rho }{{{\in }_{0}}}$

$\therefore \left( \frac{\partial \text{E}}{\partial \text{x}} \right)+\left( \frac{\partial \text{E}}{\partial \text{y}} \right)+\left( \frac{\partial \text{E}}{\partial \text{z}} \right)=\frac{\rho }{{{\epsilon }_{0}}}$

(Differential form of Gauss law) In terms of potential :

$-\left[ \frac{{{\partial }^{2}}V}{\partial {{x}^{2}}}+\frac{{{\partial }^{2}}V}{\partial {{y}^{2}}}+\frac{{{\partial }^{2}}V}{\partial {{z}^{2}}} \right]=\frac{\rho }{{{\epsilon }_{0}}}$

In other forms

$\vec{\nabla }=\left( \frac{\partial }{\partial \text{x}} \right)\overset{}{\mathop{\text{i}}}\,+\left( \frac{\partial }{\partial \text{y}} \right)\overset{}{\mathop{\text{j}}}\,+\left( \frac{\partial }{\partial \text{z}} \right)\overset{}{\mathop{\text{k}}}\,$

$\therefore$ Differential form of Gauss's law can also be stated as :

$\vec{\nabla }\cdot \vec{E}=\frac{\rho }{{{\epsilon }_{0}}}\left( \text{ Divergence }\text{ of }\vec{E}=\frac{\rho }{{{\epsilon }_{0}}} \right)$

concepts.title: Properties of Conductors
concepts.conceptBody:
Properties of Conductors

(1) When an external charge is given to the conductor, the entire charge appears over the surface of the conductor and the entire volume of the conductor is electrically neutral.

In the absence of an external field and charge around the conductor, the surface charge density of the charge distribution over the conductor surface is inversely proportional to the radius of curvature.

$\sigma \alpha \frac{1}{\text{R}}$ (flater portion has lesser charge density than curved part.)

Note : This is applicable only when external field and charges are absent

(2) The potential at any point inside (or on the surface) of the conductor is constant since the conductor is an equipotential body.

(3) Electric field is zero inside the conductor

Hence, the volume of the conductor is electrically neutral

(4) The field intensity near the surface of the conductor is given by : $\text{E}=\frac{\sigma }{{{\epsilon }_{0}}}$ (normal to surface of the conductor)

Derivation :

Consider a Gaussin surface near the surface of the conductor as shown

$E\left( ds \right)=\frac{\left[ \sigma \left( ds \right) \right]}{{{C}_{0}}}[\sigma :$ surface density of the point $]$

$\therefore \text{ E}=\left( \frac{\sigma }{{{\epsilon }_{0}}} \right)(\vec{E}$ is perpendicular to the surface $)$

Electric field and field lines are normal to the surface of a conductor 

Net field inside a conductor is zero. It implies that no field lines enter a conductor. On the surface of a conductor, electric field and hence field lines are normal to the surface of the conductor.

If a conducting box is immersed in a uniform electric 
 


field, the field lines near the box are somewhat distorted. Similarly if a conductor is positively charged, the field lines originate from the surface and are normal at every point and if it is negatively charged the field lines terminate on the surface normally at every point.

Cavity inside a conductor 

Consider a charge $+q$ suspended in a cavity in a conductor. Consider a Gaussian surface just outside the cavity and inside the conductor $\vec{E}=0$ on this Gaussian surface as it is inside the conductor. Hence from Gauss's law, $\oint \vec{E}\cdot \text{d}\vec{S}=\frac{{{\text{q}}_{\text{in}}}}{{{\epsilon }_{0}}}$ gives ${{\text{q}}_{\text{in}}}=0$ as $\{\text{E}=0$ inside conductor$\}$

(a)

(b)

This concludes that a charge of $-q$ must reside on the metal surface of the cavity so that sum of this induced charge $-q$ and the original charge $+q$ within the Guassian surface is zero. In other words, a charge $q$ suspended inside a cavity in a conductor induces an equal and opposite charge $q$ on the surface of the cavity. Further as the conductor is electrically neutral a charge $+q$ is induced on the outer surface of the conductor. As field inside the conductor is zero the field lines coming from q cannot penetrate into the conductor. The field lines will be as shown in figure (b).

The same line of approach can be used to show that the field inside the cavity of a conductor is zero when no charge is suspended in it.

Electrostatic shielding

Suppose we have a very sensitive electronic instrument that we want to protect from external electric fields that might cause wrong measurements. We surround the instrument with a conducting box or we keep the instrument inside the cavity of a conductor. By doing this charge in the conductor is so distributed that the net electric field inside the cavity becomes zero and the instrument is protected from the external fields. This is called electrostatic shielding. The potential of a charge conductor throughout its volume is same

In any region in which $\vec{E}=0$ at all points, such as the region vary far from all charges or the interior of a charged conductor, the line integral of $\vec{E}$ is zero along any path. It means that the potential difference between any two points in the conductor are at the same potential or the interior of a charged conductor is an equipotential region.

concepts.title: Electric Field and Potential Due to Uniform Hollow Sphere
concepts.conceptBody:
Electric Field and Potential Due to Uniform Hollow Sphere

At all points inside the charged spherical conductor or hollow spherical shell, electric field $\vec{E}=\text{O}$, as there is no charge inside such a sphere. In an isolated charged spherical conductor any excess charge on it is distributed uniformly over its outer surface same as that of charged spherical shell or hollow sphere. The field at external points has the same symmetry as that of a point charge. We can construct a Gaussian surface (a sphere) of radius $r>R$. At all points of this sphere the magnitude of electric field is the same and its direction is perpendicular to the surface. Thus, we can apply

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}}  \text{ES} & ~=\frac{{{\text{q}}_{\text{in }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}}{{{\epsilon }_{0}}} \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ E}\left( 4\pi {{\text{r}}^{2}} \right) & ~=\frac{\text{q}}{{{\epsilon }_{0}}} \\ \therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ E} & ~=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{{{\text{r}}^{2}}} \\ \end{array}$

Hence, the electric field at any external point is the same as if the total charge is concentrated at centre.

At the surface of sphere $r=R$,

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ E}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{{{\text{r}}^{2}}}$

Thus, we can write,

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & {{\text{E}}_{\text{inside }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=0 \\ {} & {{\text{E}}_{\text{surface }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{{{\text{R}}^{2}}} \\ {} & {{\text{E}}_{\text{outside }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{{{\text{r}}^{2}}} \\ \end{array}$

The variation of electric field $\left( \text{E} \right)$ with the distance from the centre (r) is shown in Fig.

Note : (i) At the surface graph is discontinuous

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ (ii) }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{E}}_{\text{surface }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{{{\text{R}}^{2}}}=\frac{\text{q}/4\pi {{\text{R}}^{2}}}{{{\epsilon }_{0}}}=\frac{\sigma }{{{\epsilon }_{0}}}$

(B) POTENTIAL : As we have seen,

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & {{\text{E}}_{\text{outside }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{{{\text{r}}^{2}}} \\ {} & ~\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \frac{-\text{d}{{\text{V}}_{\text{outside }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}}{\text{dr}} \right)=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{{{\text{r}}^{2}}} \\ {} & ~\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\text{V}}\text{d}{{\text{V}}_{\text{outside }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\frac{-\text{q}}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}}\mathop{\int }_{\infty }^{\text{r}}\frac{\text{dr}}{{{\text{r}}^{2}}} \\ {} & ~\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ V}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{\text{r}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ V}\propto \frac{1}{\text{r}} \\ \end{array}$

Thus, at external points the potential at any point is the same when the whole charge is assumed to be concentrated at the centre. At the surface of the sphere, $r=R$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ V}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{\text{R}}$

At some internal point electric field is zero everywhere, therefore, the potential is same at all points which is equal to the potential at surface. Thus, we can write,

and

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & {{\text{V}}_{\text{inside }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}={{\text{V}}_{\text{surface }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{\text{R}} \\ {} & {{\text{V}}_{\text{outside }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{\text{r}} \\ \end{array}$

The potential (V) varies with the distance from the centre (r) as shown in Fig. below.

concepts.title: Electric Field and Potential Due to Solid Sphere
concepts.conceptBody:
Electric Field and Potential Due to Solid Sphere 

(a) Electric Field

Positive charge $q$ is uniformly distributed throughout the volume of a solid sphere of radius R. For finding the electric field at a distance $\text{r}(<\text{R})$ from the centre let us choose as our Gaussian surface a sphere of radius $r$, concentric with the charge distribution. From symmetry the magnitude E of electric field has the same value at every point on the Gaussian surface, and the direction of $\vec{E}$ is radial at every point on the surface. So, applying Gauss's law,

$\text{ES}=\frac{{{\text{q}}_{\text{in}}}}{{{\epsilon }_{0}}}$

Here, $S=4\pi {{r}^{2}}$ and ${{q}_{in}}=\left( \rho \right)\left( \frac{4}{3}\pi {{r}^{3}} \right)$

here, $\rho =$ charge per unit volume $=\frac{q}{\frac{4}{3}\pi {{R}^{3}}}$

Substituting these values in Eq. (i)

We have, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\mathbf{E}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{{{\mathbf{R}}^{3}}}\cdot \text{r}$

or $\text{E}\propto r$ At the centre $r=0$, so $\text{E}=0$

At surface $r=R$, So $\text{ E}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{{{\text{R}}^{2}}}$

To find the electric field outside the charged sphere, we use a spherical Gaussian surface of radius $r(>R)$. This surface encloses the entire charged sphere, so ${{q}_{in}}=q$, and Gauss's law gives

$\text{E}\left( 4\pi {{\text{r}}^{2}} \right)=\frac{\text{q}}{{{\epsilon }_{0}}}\text{ or }\text{ E}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{{{\text{r}}^{2}}}$

or $\text{E}\propto \frac{1}{{{\text{r}}^{2}}}$

Notice that if we set $\text{r}=\text{R}$ in either of the two expressions for $\text{E}$ (outside and inside the sphere), we get the same result,

$\text{E}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{1}{{{\text{R}}^{2}}}$

this is because $\text{E}$ is continuous function of $\text{r}$ in this case. By contrast, for the charged conducting sphere the magnitude of electric field is discontinuous at $\text{r}=\text{R}$ (it jumps from $\text{E}=0$ to $\text{E}=\sigma /{{\epsilon }_{0}}$ ).

Thus, for a uniformly charged solid sphere we have the following formulae for magnitude of electric field.

$\begin{array}{l} {{\text{E}}_{\text{inside }}}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{{{\text{R}}^{3}}}\cdot \text{r} \\ {{\text{E}}_{\text{surface }}}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{{{\text{R}}^{2}}} \\ {{\text{E}}_{\text{outside }}}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{{{\text{r}}^{2}}} \\ \end{array}$

The variation of electric field (E) with the distance from the centre of the sphere $\left( \text{r} \right)$ is shown in Fig. below.

(B) Potential

The field intensity outside the sphere is,

$\begin{array}{l} {{\text{E}}_{\text{outside }}}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{{{\text{r}}^{2}}} \\ \frac{\text{d}{{\text{V}}_{\text{outside }}}}{\text{dr}}=-{{\text{E}}_{\text{outside }}} \\ \therefore \text{ d}{{\text{V}}_{\text{outside }}}=-{{\text{E}}_{\text{outside }}}\text{dr} \\ \text{ or } \mathop{\int }_{\infty }^{\text{V}}\text{dV}{{\text{V}}_{\text{outside }}}=-\mathop{\int }_{\infty }^{\text{r}}\frac{1}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{{{\text{r}}^{2}}}\text{dr} \\ \therefore \text{V}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{\text{r}}\text{ as }{{\text{V}}_{\infty }}=0 \\ \text{ or } \text{V}\propto \frac{1}{\text{r}} \\ \text{ At }r=R\text{, } \\ \text{V}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{\text{R}} \\ \end{array}$

i.e., at the surface of the sphere potential is ${{\text{V}}_{\text{S}}}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{\text{R}}$

The electric intensity inside the sphere,

$\begin{array}{l} {{\text{E}}_{\text{inside }}}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{{{\text{R}}^{3}}}\cdot \text{r} \\ \frac{d{{V}_{\text{inside }}}}{dr}=-{{E}_{\text{inside }}} \\ \text{d}{{\text{V}}_{\text{inside }}}=-{{\text{E}}_{\text{inside }}}\text{dr} \\ \mathop{\int }_{{{V}_{\text{S}}}}^{\text{V}}\text{d}{{\text{V}}_{\text{inside }}}=-\frac{1}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{{{\text{R}}^{3}}}\mathop{\int }_{\text{R}}^{\text{r}}\text{r}d\text{r} \\ \text{V}-{{\text{V}}_{\text{S}}}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{{{\text{R}}^{3}}}\left[ \frac{{{\text{r}}^{2}}}{2} \right]_{\text{R}}^{\text{r}} \\ \end{array}$

Substituting ${{\text{V}}_{\text{S}}}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{\text{R}}$, we get $\text{V}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}}\frac{\text{q}}{\text{R}}\left[ \frac{3}{2}-\frac{1}{2}\frac{{{\text{r}}^{2}}}{{{\text{R}}^{2}}} \right]$

At the centre $r=0$ and ${{V}_{c}}=\frac{3}{2}\left( \frac{1}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{q}{R} \right)=\frac{3}{2}{{V}_{S}}$ i.e., potential at the centre is 1.5 times the potential at surface. Thus, for a uniformly charged solid sphere we have the following formulae for potential.

and

$\begin{array}{l} {{\text{V}}_{\text{outside }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{\text{r}} \\ {{\text{V}}_{\text{surface }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{\text{R}} \\ {{\text{V}}_{\text{inside }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{\text{R}}\left[ \frac{3}{2}-\frac{1}{2}\frac{{{\text{r}}^{2}}}{{{\text{R}}^{2}}} \right] \\ \end{array}$

The variation of potential $\left( \text{V} \right)$ with distance from the centre $\left( \text{r} \right)$ is as shown in figure.

concepts.title: Some Important Points
concepts.conceptBody:
Points to Remember 

1.To find the electric potential due to a conducting sphere (or shell) we should keep in mind the following two points :

(i) Electric potential on the surface and at any point inside the sphere is

$\text{V}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{\text{R}}$

$(\text{R}=$ radius of sphere $)$

(ii) Electric potential at any point outside the sphere is

$\text{V}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}\cdot \frac{\text{q}}{\text{r}}\left( \text{r}=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ distance }\!\!~\!\!\text{ of }\!\!~\!\!\text{ the }\!\!~\!\!\text{ point }\!\!~\!\!\text{ from }\!\!~\!\!\text{ the }\!\!~\!\!\text{ centre }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \right)$

For example, in the figure shown, potential at A is

${{\text{V}}_{\text{A}}}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}\left[ \frac{{{\text{q}}_{\text{A}}}}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{A}}}}+\frac{{{\text{q}}_{\text{B}}}}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{B}}}}+\frac{{{\text{q}}_{\text{C}}}}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{C}}}} \right]$

Similarly, potential at B is

${{\text{V}}_{\text{B}}}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}}\left[ \frac{{{\text{q}}_{\text{A}}}}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{B}}}}+\frac{{{\text{q}}_{\text{B}}}}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{B}}}}+\frac{{{\text{q}}_{\text{C}}}}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{C}}}} \right]$

and potential $\text{C}$ is,

  1. Principle of a generator : A generator is an instrument for producing high voltages in the million volt region. Its design is based on the principle that if a charged conductor (say A) is brought into contact with a hollow conductor (say B), all of its charge transfers to the hollow conductor no matter how high the potential of the latter may be. This can be shown as under.

In the figure,

and $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{V}}_{\text{B}}}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}}\left[ \frac{{{\text{q}}_{\text{A}}}}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{B}}}}-\frac{{{\text{q}}_{\text{B}}}}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{B}}}} \right]$

${{\text{V}}_{\text{A}}}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}}\left[ \frac{{{\text{q}}_{\text{A}}}}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{A}}}}-\frac{{{\text{q}}_{\text{B}}}}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{B}}}} \right]$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{V}}_{\text{A}}}-{{\text{V}}_{\text{B}}}=\frac{{{\text{q}}_{\text{A}}}}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}}\left[ \frac{1}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{A}}}}-\frac{1}{{{\text{r}}_{\text{B}}}} \right]$

From this expression following conclusions can be drawn :

(i) The potential difference (PD) depends on ${{\text{q}}_{\text{A}}}$ only. It does not depend on ${{\text{q}}_{\text{B}}}$.

(ii) If ${{\text{q}}_{\text{A}}}$ is positive, then ${{\text{V}}_{\text{A}}}-{{\text{V}}_{\text{B}}}$ is positive (as ${{\text{r}}_{\text{A}}}<{{\text{r}}_{\text{B}}}$ ), i.e., ${{\text{V}}_{\text{A}}}$ $>{{\text{V}}_{\text{B}}}$. So if the two spheres are connected by a conducting wire charge flows from inner sphere to outer sphere (positive charge flows from higher potential to lower potential) till ${{\text{V}}_{\text{A}}}={{\text{V}}_{\text{B}}}$ or ${{\text{V}}_{\text{A}}}-{{\text{V}}_{\text{B}}}=0$. And potential difference will become zero only when ${{\text{q}}_{\text{A}}}=0$, i.e., all charge ${{\text{q}}_{\text{A}}}$ flows from inner sphere to


outer sphere.

(iii) If ${{\text{q}}_{\text{A}}}$ is negative, ${{\text{V}}_{\text{A}}}-{{\text{V}}_{\text{B}}}$ is negative, i.e. ${{\text{V}}_{\text{A}}}<{{\text{V}}_{\text{B}}}$. Hence, when the two spheres are connected by a thin wire all charge ${{\text{q}}_{\text{A}}}$ will flow from inner sphere to the outer sphere. Because negative charge flows from lower potential to higher potential. Thus, we see that the whole charge ${{q}_{A}}$ flows from inner sphere to outer sphere, no matter how high ${{q}_{B}}$ is. Charge always flows from $A$ to $B$, whether ${{q}_{A}}>{{q}_{B}}$ or ${{q}_{B}}>{{q}_{A}},{{V}_{A}}>{{V}_{B}}$ or ${{V}_{B}}>{{V}_{A}}$.

  1. Earthing a conductor : Potential of earth is often taken to be zero. If a conductor is connected to the earth, the potential of the conductor becomes equal to that of the earth, i.e., zero. If the conductor was at some other potential, charges will flow from it to the earthor from the earth to it to bring its potential to zero.
  2. Charges appearing on different surfacesof concentric spherical shells : Figure shows three concentric thin spherical shells A, B and C of radii a, b and c. The shells A and C are given charges ${{q}_{1}}$ and ${{q}_{2}}$ and the shell $B$ is earthed. We are interested in finding the charges on inner and outer surfaces of A, B and C. To solve such type of problems following points should keep in mind : 
     

(i) The whole charge ${{q}_{1}}$ will come on the outer surface of A unless some charge is kept inside A.

To understand it let us consider a Guassian surface (a sphere) through the material of A. As the electric field in a conducting material is zero. The flux through this Gaussian surface is zero. Using Gauss's law, the total charge enclosed must be zero.

(ii) Similarly if we draw a Gaussian surface through the material of B we can see that

${{\text{q}}_{3}}+{{\text{q}}_{1}}=0\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{q}}_{3}}=-{{\text{q}}_{1}}$

and if we draw a Gaussian surface through the material of $\text{C}$, then

${{\text{q}}_{5}}+{{\text{q}}_{4}}+{{\text{q}}_{3}}+{{\text{q}}_{1}}=0\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{q}}_{5}}=-{{\text{q}}_{4}}$

(iii) ${{q}_{5}}+{{q}_{6}}={{q}_{2}}$. As ${{q}_{2}}$ charge was given to shell $C$.

(iv) Potential of $\text{B}$ should be zero, as it is earthed. Thus,

or

$\frac{1}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}}\left[ \frac{{{\text{q}}_{1}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ b}}+\frac{{{\text{q}}_{3}}+{{\text{q}}_{4}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ b}}+\frac{{{\text{q}}_{5}}+{{\text{q}}_{6}}}{\text{c}} \right]=0$

So, using the above conditions we can find charges on different surfaces.

We can summarise the above points as under

(1) Net charge inside a closed Gaussian surface drawn in any shell is zero. (provided the shell is conducting).

(2) Potential of the conductor which is earthed is zero. (3) If two conductors are connected, they are at same potential.

(4) Charge remains constant in all conductors except those which are earthed.

(5) Charge on the inner surface of the innermost shell is zero provided no charge is kept inside it. In all other shells charge resides on both the surfaces.

(6) Equal and opposite charges appear on opposite faces.

concepts.title: Shielding Effect
concepts.conceptBody:
Shielding Effect

(1) If an external charge(s)/field are present in the surrounding of an electrical conductor then charge distribution on the surface of the conductor occurs in such a way that the net field intensity, due to the surrounding charge/field and the charge distributions (due to the surrounding charge) developed on the conductors surface; at any point inside the conductor becomes zero.

(2) If the external charge in the surrounding is arbitarily displaced/or the field charged; 


then re-distributing of charge occurs on the surface of the conductor at once, so that net field intensity at any point inside the conductor still remains zero.

(3) If a cavity is created inside the electrical conductor, then electric field inside the cavity still remains zero (in the absence of external charge in the cavity).

Reason : To satisfy $\oint \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\vec{E}\cdot \overrightarrow{\text{dl}}=0,\vec{E}$ must zero inside the cavity.

(4) Charge placed in the cavity in a conductor :

When an external charge is placed inside the cavity of a conductor, the equal and opposite charge distributian is induced on the surface of the cavity, so that the field intensity due to both the external charge in the cavity and the induced charge at any point outside the cavity is zero.

${\vec{E}_{\text{inside }\!\!~\!\!\text{ conductor }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}={{\vec{E}}_{\text{inner }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}+{{\vec{E}}_{\text{outer }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=0+0=0$

(5) If the external charge in the cavity is arbitarily displaced, then, re-distribution of induced charge on the surface of cavity takes place so that field intensity outside the cavity still remains zero.

(6) Outer charge distribution remain unchanged if the external charge in the cavity is arbitarily displaced and the inner charge distribution remains unchanged if the outer charge distribution charges (i.e. vice-versa). 

(7) Electric field at any point (inside the cavity) near its surface $=\left( \frac{\sigma }{{{\epsilon }_{0}}} \right)$. As a general idea, field is more strong at a point where, charge is closer to surface of the cavity.

(8) The electric potential, due to the external charge and the induced charge on the surface of the cavity, at any point OUTSIDE THE CAVITY IS ZERO.

(Reason : No work done against field as $\text{E}=$ due to both)

Remember : ELECTRIC POTENTIAL IS NOT ZERO due to the outer charge distribution inside the cavity.

(9) The net force experienced by any external charge inside the cavity is zero.

Proof : 

Assume a Gaussian surface near the charge

$\oint \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\vec{E}\cdot \overrightarrow{\text{ds}}=\frac{\left( {{\text{q}}_{\text{inside }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}} \right)}{{{\in }_{0}}}$

$\therefore {{\vec{E}}_{\text{ext }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=0[\because \text{q}=0$ excluding the charge itself$]$

$\left(\vec{F}=0 \right)$

(10) Hence, a conducting shell divide the entire regions into two parts, inner and outer which are completely independent of each other, in terms of electrostatic field. This is called as the shielding effect in the electrical conductors.

concepts.title: Parallel Plates
concepts.conceptBody:
Parallel Plates

Two important concepts to be applied to all questions based on the parallel plates :

(1) The charges appearing on the faces opposite to each combination of pair of parallel plates are always equal is magnitudes and opposite in nature.

(2) The outer faces of the extreme plates have equal charges is magnitude and nature and equal to the (sum of charges on all plates divided by two) charge on the outer plates faces $($outer$)=\frac{\sum {{\text{Q}}_{\text{all}}}}{2}$

Derivation 

(1) Use Gauss's law

Draw a Guasssian surface ABCD

Now $\oint \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\vec{E}\cdot \text{d} \vec{s}=\left( \frac{{{\text{q}}_{\text{inside }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}}{{{\text{C}}_{0}}} \right)$

$\oint {{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}_{ABCD}}\vec{E}\cdot d\vec{s}={{\int }_{AB}}\vec{E}\cdot d\vec{s}+{{\int }_{BC}}\vec{E}\cdot d\vec{s}+{{\int }_{CD}}\vec{E}\cdot ds+{{\int }_{OA}}\vec{E}\cdot ds$

$\left. =\underset{\left[ \because \text{E}\bot \text{ds} \right]}{\mathop{0}}\,+\underset{[\vec{E}=0}{\mathop{0}}\,{{]}^{+}}{{\left[ \begin{matrix} 0 \\ \vec{E}\bot \text{ds} \\ \end{matrix} \right]}^{+}}\begin{matrix} 0 \\ \vec{E}=0 \\ \end{matrix} \right]=0$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Net charge enclosed $=0$

(2) To prove 2nd, use the concept that $\text{E}=0$ inside the conductor which can happen only when the outer faces of the extreme plate have equal charges is magnitude as well as in direction.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \frac{\left( {{\text{Q}}_{1}}-\text{q} \right)}{2{{\in }_{0}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A}} & ~=\frac{\left( {{\text{Q}}_{2}}+\text{q} \right)}{2{{\in }_{0}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A}} \\ \text{q} & ~=\frac{\left( {{\text{Q}}_{1}}-{{\text{Q}}_{2}} \right)}{2} \\ \therefore {{\text{q}}_{\text{outer }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ on }\!\!~\!\!\text{ each }\!\!~\!\!\text{ plate }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & ~={{\text{Q}}_{1}}-\frac{\left( {{\text{Q}}_{1}}-{{\text{Q}}_{2}} \right)}{2}=\left( \frac{{{\text{Q}}_{1}}+{{\text{Q}}_{2}}}{2} \right) \\ \end{array}$
